content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Your bank’s app probably has a chat assistant in it now. You might have ignored it, or tapped it once and then closed it when you weren’t sure what to ask. A lot of people do. But once you know what it’s good at, it can save you real time. And once you know its limits, you’ll know exactly when to talk to a person instead.
This guide covers both sides.
In 2026, Visa announced an AI Financial Assistant that lets cardholders ask plain-English questions about their own transactions, lock their card, and set spending alerts — all inside their existing bank app. (Source: Visa, 2026.) Major banks have been adding similar features of their own, and what all of these have in common is the same basic idea: instead of hunting through menus, you just ask.
The technology is the same kind of AI that powers tools like ChatGPT — it reads your question, interprets what you mean, and pulls the relevant information from your account. The key difference from a general chatbot is that your bank’s assistant is connected to your actual transaction data. It knows what you spent, where, and when.
Finding a charge you don’t recognise. This is where it shines. Instead of scrolling through months of statements, you can describe what you’re looking for: “Show me all charges from a company called ‘Adobe’ in the past six months” or “I see a charge for £14.99 on 12 July — what is it?” The assistant pulls up the exact transaction and shows you the full merchant name, which is often longer and more recognisable than the shortened version on your statement.
Explaining a fee. Banks charge fees that feel mysterious — monthly maintenance fees, out-of-network ATM fees, foreign transaction fees. Ask: “Why was I charged $3.50 on 5 July?” It can usually tell you exactly what the fee was for and what you can do to avoid it in future.
Showing your spending by category. Most bank assistants can produce a quick summary: “How much did I spend on restaurants last month?” or “What were my top five spending categories this year?” These aren’t as detailed as a dedicated budgeting app, but for a quick picture, they’re useful.
Setting alerts. You can ask the assistant to notify you when your balance drops below a certain amount, when any charge over a certain size goes through, or when a specific recurring payment is taken. These alerts can catch problems early.
Locking your card. If you can’t find your debit card, you don’t have to call anyone. Most bank assistants can lock it immediately. If you find the card later, you can unlock it the same way. Quick and done.
The assistant understands plain English, but it responds better to specific questions than vague ones.
Use dates or amounts when you have them. “Show me charges from May” is easier for it to handle than “show me old charges.”
Describe what you remember. “A charge I don’t recognise” is less useful than “a charge for about £20 from a name starting with ‘S’.”
Ask one thing at a time. Combining several requests in one message often confuses any chat system. Take it step by step.
Here are examples of questions that work well:
“What did I spend at Tesco in June?”
“I see a charge for $9.99 labeled ‘VZWRLSS’ — what is that?”
“Lock my debit card. I’ll unlock it when I find it.”
The assistant is better at looking things up than at giving you opinions. Keep that in mind and you’ll find it much more useful.
I use the bank assistant mainly to settle one question: whether a subscription I think I cancelled actually stopped. I’ll ask “Show me all charges from Hulu in the last 12 months” and the answer is right there — no scrolling through statements, no phone call needed. It saves a surprising amount of time once you know to ask.
This is the part that matters most for your financial safety.
It cannot give you reliable financial advice. An MIT Sloan finance expert notes that AI chatbots work on probability patterns, not exact math — which makes them unreliable for precise financial calculations. (Source: MIT Sloan.) Asking the assistant “Should I pay off my credit card or put the money in savings?” or “Will I have enough to retire at 65?” will get you an answer. That answer may sound confident and thorough. Do not act on it without speaking to a licensed human adviser.
It has no fiduciary duty. A licensed financial adviser is legally required to act in your best interest. The bank’s AI assistant is not. It has no legal responsibility for bad advice. (Source: MIT Sloan.) This doesn’t mean it’s trying to mislead you — it isn’t — but it does mean there is no recourse if it gets something important wrong.
Its math can be off. For precise calculations — how much interest you’ll pay over five years, what your net income is after a specific deduction — do not rely on the assistant alone. Verify the numbers yourself or ask a person.
It cannot resolve disputes or fraud claims. The assistant can sometimes start a dispute process. The actual resolution requires a human. Ask it to connect you to a fraud specialist or dispute team, then follow through with a real person.
Your bank’s AI assistant has access to your account data — the same information you see when you log in: your balance, your transaction history, your account numbers. In most cases it can read that data, but it cannot move money without your explicit confirmation step.
What it does not have access to is your broader financial life. It cannot see accounts at other banks, your investment portfolio, your credit score, or your tax history (unless those are explicitly linked to that specific bank).
This matters because when the assistant gives you a picture of your finances, it is an incomplete picture. It only sees what is inside its own walls.
Impostor “bank AI” messages. Scammers now send texts, emails, and messages that pretend to be your bank’s AI assistant. They look official. They create urgency. They ask you to confirm your password, your card number, or a verification code.
Your real bank AI will never ask for any of those things. If a message asks you to “verify your credentials” or “confirm your details to continue,” close it immediately. Open your bank’s official app directly — not through any link in the message — and report the contact.
Over-trusting the numbers. The assistant can produce figures that look precise and authoritative — a projection of how your savings will grow, an estimate of fees you’ll face. These figures can be wrong. Treat any calculation from the assistant as a rough guide, not a definitive answer.
Oversharing. The assistant already knows your account data. Some people go further and share things it doesn’t need: their full national insurance or Social Security number, their online banking password, or security question answers. The assistant has no legitimate reason to ask for any of these. If it seems to be asking, stop the conversation and call your bank directly.
Assuming the assistant speaks for the bank officially. The assistant might explain a fee, a policy, or a rule. That explanation might be wrong or out of date. If something matters — whether you qualify for a fee waiver, what the early-closure penalty is — confirm it with a human representative.
If you want to understand how your bank’s AI protects your money behind the scenes — not the assistant you talk to, but the fraud detection running on every purchase — How Your Bank Uses AI to Protect Your Money covers that in detail.
And if you’re interested in using AI to actively manage your budget rather than just look things up, Best AI Budgeting Apps: What They Do and What to Expect is the natural next read.
